_concept: Photosynthesis is the biochemical process by which plants convert sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide into glucose and oxygen in chloroplasts.
_purpose: Third concept for N-axiom contrastive testing.
positives: Photosynthesis converts sunlight into chemical energy that plants store as glucose.
In every leaf, chloroplasts run photosynthesis whenever direct light reaches the plant.
Without photosynthesis the global oxygen supply would collapse within a few years.
Botany students learn that photosynthesis takes place inside the chloroplasts of green tissue.
Carbon dioxide and water flow into a leaf, and through photosynthesis they leave as glucose and oxygen.
Most life on Earth depends on the oxygen that photosynthesis releases into the atmosphere.
The light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis occur on the thylakoid membranes.
Photosynthesis slows dramatically when stomata close during drought stress.
Algae perform photosynthesis just as land plants do, contributing the bulk of marine oxygen.
Greenhouse growers sometimes raise carbon dioxide levels to accelerate photosynthesis.
The Calvin cycle is the dark-reaction half of photosynthesis, fixing CO2 into sugars.
Chlorophyll's green colour is what lets photosynthesis capture energy from blue and red light.
Net photosynthesis falls below zero on heavily overcast winter days.
Discoverers slowly worked out that photosynthesis releases the oxygen we breathe, not the carbon.
Photosynthesis evolved more than two billion years ago and reshaped Earth's atmosphere.
Indoor plants grow slowly because the artificial light they receive limits photosynthesis.
C4 plants have a specialised photosynthesis pathway that resists photorespiration in heat.
When biology classes measure bubbles from elodea, they are watching photosynthesis in real time.
Crop yields hinge on the rate of photosynthesis during the growing season's peak weeks.
Photosynthesis is endothermic; it stores energy from photons in the bonds of glucose.
Forest canopies dominate global photosynthesis because they intercept the most sunlight.
Not all photosynthesis produces oxygen — anoxygenic versions exist in certain bacteria.
Models of photosynthesis at the leaf scale feed into climate predictions about carbon sinks.
Cherry blossoms ramp up photosynthesis the moment temperatures rise in early spring.
Photosynthesis can be reverse-engineered by tracking radiolabelled CO2 through a leaf.
On bright clear afternoons, photosynthesis lifts atmospheric oxygen near forest floors.
Engineers studying photosynthesis hope to one day build artificial leaves for solar fuel.
Cellular respiration is essentially photosynthesis run in reverse: glucose plus O2 yielding energy plus CO2.
Long days near the poles allow photosynthesis to continue almost around the clock in summer.
Photosynthesis ceases entirely once a leaf detaches and dries out, no matter how much light it gets.
